solution:
Given,

A 5×5 matrix whose each entry is either 0 or 1, is such that sum of entries of each column as well as each row is 1,

Taking a possible case we get,

1000001000001000001000001

Now arranging the above case we get,

1000001000001000001000001→5 ways→4 ways→3 ways→2 ways→1 way because if we fix 1 at first row and first column then in second row first column 1 cannot come and vice-versa.

So total number of such matrices will be 5×4×3×2×1=120

q: Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -5\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 4\alpha & -2\alpha \end{bmatrix} + \text{adj}(A)$. If $\det(B)=66$, then $\det(\text{adj}(A))$ equals:
question: Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -5\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 4\alpha & -2\alpha \end{bmatrix} + \text{adj}(A)$. If $\det(B)=66$, then $\det(\text{adj}(A))$ equals:
solution:
The cofactor matrix of $A$ is calculated as follows:

$C_{11} = 15$, $C_{12} = -10$, $C_{13} = 8$

$C_{21} = 3$, $C_{22} = 5\alpha$, $C_{23} = -4\alpha$

$C_{31} = -6$, $C_{32} = 4$, $C_{33} = 3\alpha - 2$

The adjoint of $A$ is the transpose of the cofactor matrix:

$\text{adj}(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 3 & -6 \\ -10 & 5\alpha & 4 \\ 8 & -4\alpha & 3\alpha - 2 \end{bmatrix}$

The matrix $B$ is given by:

$B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -5\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 4\alpha & -2\alpha \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 3 & -6 \\ -10 & 5\alpha & 4 \\ 8 & -4\alpha & 3\alpha - 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 16 & 3 & -6 \\ -10 & 0 & 4 \\ 8 & 0 & \alpha - 2 \end{bmatrix}$

Expanding the determinant of $B$ along the second column:

$\det(B) = -3 \begin{vmatrix} -10 & 4 \\ 8 & \alpha - 2 \end{vmatrix} = -3(-10(\alpha - 2) - 32) = -3(-10\alpha - 12) = 30\alpha + 36$

Given $\det(B) = 66$, we get:

$30\alpha + 36 = 66 \Rightarrow 30\alpha = 30 \Rightarrow \alpha = 1$

The determinant of $A$ is:

$\det(A) = \alpha(15 - 0) - 1(10 - 0) + 2(8 - 0) = 15\alpha + 6$

Substituting $\alpha = 1$:

$\det(A) = 15(1) + 6 = 21$

Using the property $\det(\text{adj}(A)) = (\det(A))^{n-1}$ for a $3 \times 3$ matrix:

$\det(\text{adj}(A)) = (\det(A))^2 = (21)^2 = 441$

Answer: $441$

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \left|\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)\left(2 \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)\right|=28 \\ & \left|\left(\mathrm{~A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)\left(2 \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)\right|=24 \\ & \left|\mathrm{~A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right|\left|2 \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right|= \pm 16 \\ & \left(\mathrm{~A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}-2 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \left|\mathrm{~A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right|=\left|\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}-2 \mathrm{~A}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\mathrm{A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right|^2=16 \\ & \left|\mathrm{~A}-2 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right|= \pm 4 \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & \alpha \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 2 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 \alpha & 2 & 4\end{array}\right]} \\ & \left|\begin{array}{lll}-1 & 0 & \alpha \\ -3 & 0 & -1 \\ -2 \alpha & -1 & -2\end{array}\right| \\ & 1+3 \alpha=4 \\ & 3 \alpha=3 \\ & \alpha=1 \\ & |\mathrm{~A}|=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right|=-1-3=-4 \\ & |\mathrm{~A}|^2=16\end{aligned}$

q: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -2 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $P=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta\end{array}\right], \theta\gt0$. If $\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{PAP}^{\mathrm{T}}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{B}^{10} \mathrm{P}$ and the sum of the diagonal elements of $C$ is $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is :
question: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -2 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $P=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta\end{array}\right], \theta\gt0$. If $\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{PAP}^{\mathrm{T}}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{B}^{10} \mathrm{P}$ and the sum of the diagonal elements of $C$ is $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right] \\ & \because \mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{~B}=\mathrm{PAPT} \end{aligned}$ Pre multiply by $\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}}$ ( Given) $\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{~B}=\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{P} \mathrm{AP}^{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{AP}^{\mathrm{T}}$ Now post multiply by P $\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{AP}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{A}$
$\mathrm{A}^2=\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{~B}^2 \mathrm{P}$ Similarly $A^{10}=P^T B^{10} P=C$ $\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \text { (Given) } \\ & \Rightarrow A^2=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{2} & -\sqrt{2}-2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$ Similarly check $A^3$ and so on since $C=A^{10}$ $\Rightarrow$ Sum of diagonal elements of C is $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{10}+1$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{32}+1=\frac{33}{32}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \operatorname{g~cd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1(\text { Given }) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=65\end{aligned}$

q: Let $A, B$ and $C$ be three $2 \times 2$ matrices with real entries such that $B=(I+A)^{-1}$ and $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{I}$. If $\mathrm{BC}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -5 \\ -1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{CB}\left[\begin{array}{l}x_{1} \\ x_{2}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}12 \\ -6\end{array}\right]$, then $x_{1}+x_{2}$ is
question: Let $A, B$ and $C$ be three $2 \times 2$ matrices with real entries such that $B=(I+A)^{-1}$ and $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{I}$. If $\mathrm{BC}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -5 \\ -1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{CB}\left[\begin{array}{l}x_{1} \\ x_{2}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}12 \\ -6\end{array}\right]$, then $x_{1}+x_{2}$ is
solution:
From $B = (I + A)^{-1}$ we get $B(I + A) = I$, so $BA = I - B$.
From $A + C = I$: $C = I - A$.
Calculating $CB = C(I+A)^{-1} = (I-A)(I+A)^{-1}$.
Since $B(I+A) = I$, we have $(I+A) = B^{-1}$, so $C = (I-A)B^{-1}$.
Multiplying by $B$: $CB = (I-A)$.
However, using the given $BC = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -5 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B(I+A) = I$ implies $B - BA = B - (I-B) = 2B - I = BC$, i.e. $BC = 2B - I$.
Therefore $B = \frac{1}{2}(BC + I) = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -5 \\ -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -5/2 \\ -1/2 & 3/2 \end{bmatrix}$.
Also $CB = 2B - I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -5 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$.
From $CB\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix}$: $(CB)^{-1} = -\frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
So $\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = -\frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$.
Therefore $x_1 + x_2 = 0$

solution:
Let $p_1 x=a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1$
$$
p_2 x=a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2
$$
and $p_3 x=a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3$
where $a_1, a_2, a_3, b_1, b_2, b_3, c_1, c_2, c_3$ are real numbers.
$$
\begin{gathered}

a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 & 2 a_3 x+b_3 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right] \\
=\left[\begin{array}{llll}

2 a_1 & 2 a_2 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right] \\

question: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & x \\ 3 & -1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{c}y \\ x \\ 1\end{array}\right]$ be such that $\mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{l}6 \\ 8\end{array}\right]$, then:
solution:
Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & x \\ 3 & -1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{l}y \\ x \\ 1\end{array}\right]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 2 & x \\
3 & -1 & 2
\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}
y \\
x \\
1
\end{array}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l}
6 \\
8
\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}
y+2 x+x \\
3 y-x+2
\end{array}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l}
6 \\
8
\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}
y+3 x \\
3 y-x+2
\end{array}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow y+3 x=6 \text { and } 3 y-x=6
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x=\frac{6}{5} \text { and } y=\frac{12}{5} \\
&\Rightarrow y=2 x
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and $B$ be a matrix such that $B(I-A)=I+A$. Then the sum of the diagonal elements of $\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{B}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and $B$ be a matrix such that $B(I-A)=I+A$. Then the sum of the diagonal elements of $\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{B}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$A$ is skew-symmetric ($A^T = -A$).
From $B(I-A) = I+A$:
$B = (I+A)(I-A)^{-1}$.
$B^T = ((I-A)^T)^{-1}(I+A)^T = (I+A)^{-1}(I-A)$.
Since $A$ is skew-symmetric, $(I-A)$ and $(I+A)$ commute:
$(I-A)(I+A) = I - A^2 = (I+A)(I-A)$.
$B^TB = (I+A)^{-1}(I-A)(I+A)(I-A)^{-1} = (I+A)^{-1}(I+A)(I-A)(I-A)^{-1} = I$.
Trace of $B^TB = \text{tr}(I) = 3$.
